entry.title: Re: How to prevent Unplanned Cross Plant Goods issue and...
entry.content: For preventing unplanned cross‑plant goods issues/receipts in MIGO (261/262), the correct place to implement the check is BADI_MMIM_CHECK_MATDOC_ITEM. The key is to refine the condition handling at item level. Compare the plant in MATDOC with the order’s plant (AUFK/AFKO), and only trigger the error when both the movement type (261/262) and the order category (production order) match.

entry.title: Re: Mov 712 in EWM - counterpart from ERP
entry.content:
MON shows warehouse tasks, warehouse orders, and stock movements relevant to EWM. A pure ERP transfer posting may not generate an EWM document number unless it triggers a warehouse process.
Ensure the ERP posting was actually sent to EWM. If the queue failed, the document won’t appear in MON.
In customizing, confirm that 712 is mapped to an EWM process type. If it is not, EWM won’t generate a corresponding document.
So in short, if you don’t see the 712 movement in MON, it is likely because the movement type is not integrated to EWM by default. You will need to check the customizing for ERP–EWM movement type mapping and ensure the queue transfer is working.

entry.title: Re: Observer Design Pattern – Real-Time Use Cases
entry.content: You can simplify pattern's syntax by creating a custom BADI. Each observer will be a BADI implementation, and your subject will use simple commands like GET BADI / CALL BADI to notify observers.

entry.title: Re: Required Fiori Usage Statistics
entry.content:
Hello,

I understand your need for a solution to track usage statistics for Fiori applications within the NW AS ABAP system. At this time, there is no dedicated tool delivered by SAP for this specific task. However, there are several resources available that outline best practices and alternative approaches to retrieve this information:

- 3203036 - Tracking usage of Fiori apps https://me.sap.com/notes/0003203036
- 2629143 - Best practices to gather Fiori usage statistics for on-premise systems https://me.sap.com/notes/0002629143

Fiori Tracker: Tiles usage and User monitoring for SAP Fiori launchpad (Monitoring SAP Fiori Launchpad)

Apps usage report for SAP Fiori launchpad (Monitoring SAP Fiori applications)

Some of these options involve custom or third-party solutions, which are not part of the standard offerings from SAP.
Additionally, there is a helpful SAP-delivered application within the SAP Cloud ALM product that may address your needs.
You can find more details about this solution at the following link:
Real User Monitoring.

Best Regards,
Joe

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud enables user-based authoriza...
entry.content:
This is achieved through a combination of:

1) Business Role Templates
Assign roles such as Sales Representative or Sales Pricing Specialist, which restrict access to specific apps and data objects.

2) Authorization Objects
Control access at the level of sales prices, pricing conditions, sales orders, and analytics reports. For example, you can restrict visibility to only the products or sales organizations the user is responsible for.

3) Analytical Privileges
Limit reporting data so users only see KPIs and analytics for their assigned product groups or sales areas.

4) Flexible Restrictions
Use organizational assignments (sales org, distribution channel, division, product responsibility) to dynamically filter what each user can access.

entry.title: Re: How to create a Tunnel connection to Datasphere in SAC?
entry.content:
Hi Julia,

 

A Tunnel Connection can be created only when SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere tenants are “deep linked.” by either of the following 3 options:

  • Option 1 — In BDC tenant where SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere tenants are linked via BDC formation
  • Option 2 — In SAP Analytics Cloud tenant and SAP Datasphere tenant (Non BDC) are linked via UCL formation. 
  • Option 3 — SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere Tenant are linked for seamless planning  (most recommended if SAC tenant is running  on HANA Cloud)

See more detail from blog, Scheduling SAP Analytics Cloud Stories Based on SA... - SAP Community

 

Best regards,
Xiwen

entry.title: Re: MF60 can not generate replenishment with 313 movement...
entry.content:
Why the second material cannot be staged with 313 is because, movement type 313 is designed for WM‑managed storage locations, where stock is transferred from WM to a non‑WM location (interim storage type to production supply area). For a material in a non‑WM managed storage location, there is no WM process behind it, so the system raises error M7 096 because 313 is not valid there. In other words, 313 only works when the issuing storage location is WM‑managed.

The standard approach is:

Use 313 for the first step which is transfer posting from WM storage location to production supply (non‑WM location). This creates the outbound delivery and triggers WM picking. Then use 261 for the second step which is goods issue from the production supply location to the production order. For materials in non‑WM storage locations, you cannot use 313. They must be staged directly with 261 (single‑step GI). If you want all materials to follow a 2‑step process, they must be assigned to WM‑managed storage locations.

Best regards,
Xiwen

entry.title: Missing Simulation in Workflow Configuration: Why PRs Deserve the Same Transparency as POs
entry.content:
With SAP S/4HANA release 2602, SAP introduced a highly welcome enhancement: the ability to simulate workflows for Purchase Orders (POs) in the app Manage Workflows for Purchase Orders. This new functionality provides much‑needed transparency by allowing users to see which workflow will be triggered for a given document and why.

 

However, when looking at the overall procurement process, one question naturally arises:

Why does this simulation capability stop at Purchase Orders?

Purchase Requisitions (PRs) are the starting point of the procurement lifecycle, yet the app Manage Workflows for Purchase Requisitions (F2705A) still lacks any comparable workflow simulation functionality. This blog post outlines why this distinction is problematic and why extending workflow simulation to Purchase Requisitions would be a meaningful and consistent improvement.

The Current Challenge: Limited Transparency in PR Workflow Determination

Workflow configuration for Purchase Requisitions is often complex:

  • Multiple workflow definitions may exist
  • Start conditions overlap
  • Different approval paths apply depending on value, material group, account assignment, or requester

Today, when a Purchase Requisition is created, the system determines the workflow in the background. If an unexpected workflow is triggered—or none at all—users and administrators are left with limited options:

  • Trial-and-error testing
  • Manual analysis of start conditions
  • Debugging or log interpretation

In contrast, the new workflow simulation for Purchase Orders allows users to proactively validate workflow behavior before real documents are processed.

This creates an inconsistency in tooling between two tightly related business objects.

Why Workflow Simulation Matters—Especially for Purchase Requisitions

Purchase Requisitions are not just another document type. They are:

  • The entry point of procurement
  • Often created by non-procurement users
  • The basis for downstream Purchase Orders and contracts

Introducing workflow simulation for PRs would address several real-world challenges:

1. Validation of Workflow Setup

Simulation would make it immediately clear:

  • Which workflow is selected
  • Which start conditions are fulfilled
  • Why one workflow is chosen over another

This is particularly valuable in systems where multiple workflows coexist.

2. Prevention of Incorrect Approvals

Without simulation, misconfigured workflows may only be discovered after release:

  • Wrong approvers
  • Missing approvals
  • Delays due to rework

Simulation enables preventive validation instead of reactive correction.

3. Reduced Dependency on Technical Analysis

Today, resolving workflow issues often requires:

  • Technical expertise
  • Backend analysis
  • IT involvement

A simulation feature empowers business users—especially purchasers—to validate workflows on their own.

Proposed Improvement

Extend the workflow simulation functionality introduced in release 2602 for Purchase Orders also to:

  • App: Manage Workflows for Purchase Requisitions
  • Fiori App ID: F2705A
  • Business Role: BR_PURCHASER

The simulation should:

  • Accept PR-relevant input parameters
  • Display the selected workflow
  • Show which start conditions are met
  • Clearly indicate why other workflows are not selected

In short: the same transparency that is now available for Purchase Orders should also be available for Purchase Requisitions.

Business Value and Benefits

For purchasing organizations, this enhancement would deliver tangible value:

✔️  Faster and safer workflow configuration

✔️  Higher process reliability

✔️  Fewer approval errors

✔️  Less troubleshooting effort

✔️  Better user confidence in workflow logic

Most importantly, it would ensure process consistency across the procurement lifecycle.

Call to Action: Help Improve Workflow Configuration

The introduction of workflow simulation for Purchase Orders in SAP S/4HANA 2602 is a strong step in the right direction. But by stopping at Purchase Orders, SAP leaves a critical gap at the very start of procurement.

If transparency and predictability are important for workflow configuration—and they are—then Purchase Requisitions deserve the same level of simulation support.

And if you have read this far, I encourage you to:

👉  Please Vote to Idea # 365219 Workflow Simulation for Purchase Requisitions on SAP Influence and help move this enhancement forward.

References

  • Workflow Simulation for Purchase Orders | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Re: Sizing the SAP Datasphere Object Store
entry.content:
Back in December / January we tested the object store and we couldn't make it perform when reading from it. We tried partitioning but it had no impact what so ever. We had a table with 500 mio rows and more than 400 columns and reading only 10 out of 400 did not work much faster as expected (parquet files are column organised). We gave up on the object store as we did not have the time to dig deeper. It would help us a lot if you (SAP) could give some expected performance measures, large tables, small tables, few columns, many columns, etc depending on number of CPUs. When we know what to expect we can also give feedback / create tickets on low performance, at the moment it is just a black box.

With kind regards

Kristian Appel

entry.title: Re: [S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition] Custom Currency Code f...
entry.content:
In Public Cloud:

1. **Can we create custom currency codes (like "USDA") in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?**
- Is this supported via standard configuration or key user extensibility?

Ans: No, you cannot create custom currency codes, neither standard nor extensibility is possible

**If custom currency codes are supported, can we maintain separate exchange rate tables for them?**

**Are there any functional limitations or restrictions when using custom currency codes?**
- Are there any standard apps, processes, or integration scenarios that do not support custom currency codes?

No, as explained in Question 1, custom currency codes are not supported.

*Is there an alternative standard approach recommended by SAP for this scenario?**
- For example, using different currency types or other configuration options?

In general companies use different exchange rate for sales tax/VAT purpose for incoming and outgoing invoices. For Vendor and Customer invoice you can use BADi to have separate exchange rate at the time of invoice generation. For all other transactions, only M rate for the exchange pair will be considered.

BADI for billing documents

Badi name : Custom Data Transfer for Billing Process Documents

ID:SD_BIL_DATA_TRANSFER

Business Contexts: SD_BILLINGDOC and SD_BILLINGDOCITEM

BADI for invoicing


Badi name: Enrich Invoicing/Posting Documents of Invoicing Unit

ID:FKK_EVENT_2645

Business Contexts:CINV_INVOICING

 

Regards

Satish

entry.title: Re: #CoE#QDGG Business function only available on 3SL.
entry.content:
Hi Jimmy,

Certain business configurations and customizations that require developer extensibility, complex localization, or advanced change management can only be fully implemented in 3SL due to its architectural advantages.

Key Distinction: The limitation is not in the business function itself, but in the implementation approach and customization depth available. 3SL enables more sophisticated adaptations of standard business processes through developer extensibility and structured testing environments.

Following please find some typical features on 3SL:

Custom Sales Order Types (Complex Logic),

Custom Pricing Procedures with Complex Calculations,

Country-Specific Tax Configurations (Non-Standard Countries),
Custom Approval Workflows with External System Integration,

Custom Production Planning Logic,

Custom Inventory Valuation Methods

 

Best regards,

Yu Yong

entry.title: Re: best-priced items in Compare Supplier Quotation app
entry.content:
The Best-Priced Items app does not just look at the lowest price in isolation. According to SAP’s logic, the determination also considers whether the quotation item can fully cover the RFQ requirement (quantity and delivery date). If a supplier offers a lower unit price but less than the requested quantity, that item will not be flagged as “best‑priced,” because it cannot fully satisfy the requirement. In your example, Supplier A quoted only 9 EA for R001 when the RFQ asked for 10 EA. Even though the price is lower, it is not considered “best‑priced” because it doesn’t meet the full requested quantity. Supplier B, on the other hand, quoted the full quantities for all three items, so all of them are marked as best‑priced, even if the price isn’t the absolute lowest in every case.

So yes, the determination logic does factor in full delivery capability at item level, not just the price. That is why you see Supplier A flagged with fewer best‑priced items than expected.

entry.title: Re: BADI for Billing document Item Custom Field
entry.content: Use BAdI SD_BIL_DATA_TRANSFER. This is the cloud‑released BAdI for billing document creation. It lets you map or overwrite fields when data is transferred from the reference document (sales order, delivery) to the billing document. You can extend the billing item CDS view with your custom fields and then populate them in the BAdI implementation. For more information, check this SAP document  Custom Data Transfer for Billing Process Documents

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud enables user-based authoriza...
entry.content:
Hello @Joey3,

in order to achieve this, you can work with Authorization Groups.

  1. These Groups need to be assigned to be set up.
  2. After the creation of Authorization Groups, they can be asisgned to Material Masters (=Products) /  Product Groups / Product Types.
  3. As a last step, you can maintain these Authorization Groups as Restrictions in app Maintain Business Roles to define which role will be able to access which elements.

 

‼️ However, in your case you mentioned Sales staff A and Sales staff B are using the same business role.

To work with Authorization Groups, you would need to set up two different business roles:

  • Business role A with access to Material 1
  • Business Role B with access to Material 2

 

Here are some links regarding the topic:

  • 2808362 - Restriction "Authorization Group for Product Group" ignored when using the App "Manage Product Master Data" - SAP for Me
  • 3203535 - Not possible to maintain values for restriction types 'Authorization Group for Product' and 'Authorization Group for Product Type' - SAP for Me

 

I hope this helps.

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: Re: best-priced items in Compare Supplier Quotation app
entry.content: If both Supplier A and Supplier B fail to meet the full RFQ quantity, then neither quotation item is considered “best‑priced” in the strict sense. The app does not select the one that is closer to the requested quantity;  it requires the full quantity to be offered. In that case, neither A (8EA) nor B (9EA) would be flagged as best‑priced for that item

entry.title: Re: PO creator should have access to return PR
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, the “return PR” functionality you are describing (similar to Oracle, where a PO creator can send a requisition back) is not available. The flexible workflow framework covers approval, rejection, and resubmission, but it does not provide a direct “return to PR” action from the PO side.

Some workarounds are

  • You can configure rejection steps in the PO workflow so that the requisition creator is notified and can adjust the PR. This is the closest equivalent to “return PR.”
  • If you need a true “return PR” button, you can try to implement an extension (via in‑app extensibility or side‑by‑side BTP extension) that triggers a workflow event to reopen the PR.

entry.title: TRM | Update Type SE1000 "Fixed" but no FI Document (Z-Copy Works) | S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

 

I am facing a strange issue in SAP Treasury and Risk Management (S/4HANA) where the standard Update Type SE1000 (Purchase) is successfully "Fixed" in the subledger, but no accounting entry is generated in FI.

 

Scenario Details:

 

Product Type: 02B (Investment in MF Debt)

 

Transaction Type: 100 (Purchase)

 

Company Code: 5200

 

Valuation Area: 001 (Operative Valuation Area - IAS)

 

The Problem:

 

When running TBB1 for a Purchase transaction (Update Type SE1000):

 

The Test Run is green and says "Test run was successful."

 

The Update Run marks the flow as "Fixed" in TPM13.

 

No FI Document is generated.

 

TPM20 (Posting Journal) is completely blank for this transaction.

 

SLG1 shows no error logs in the database.

 

SM13 shows no update terminations.

 

What I have verified:

 

Account Determination: Assigned and correct. Account symbols have valid G/L mappings.

 

Posting Relevance: SE1000 is marked as "Relevant for Posting" in SPRO.

 

FI Side: Periods are open (OB52) and Number Ranges are maintained (FBN1).

 

AAR: Account Assignment Reference is correctly maintained on the Administration tab.

 

The "Z" Breakthrough:

 

I created a custom Update Type ZSE1000 as a copy of SE1000 with the exact same settings. When using ZSE1000, the system successfully generates the posting log and FI document.

 

Questions:

 

Why does the standard SE1000 skip the accounting interface and go directly to "Fixed" status while a Z-copy works?

 

Is there a technical classification for standard update types that might suppress FI posting for SE1000?

 

Any insights or suggestions on where to look further would be greatly appreciated.

 

Note- For same product and setting, sale (200) working fine but not purchase  (100)

entry.title: Re: HANA does not retain table partitioning when a column...
entry.content:
Please refer the details from KBA 2044468  - FAQ: SAP HANA Partitioning ->

19. Is the partitioning information kept during transports?

Partitioning information is not available in ABAP DDIC. Therefore transports don't consider it and partitioning is not transported. This means that you have to activate partitioning in all involved systems of your ABAP system landscape individually.

Hope this is helpful.

~Gaurav

With AI embedded directly into sales, service, commerce, and engagement workflows, SAP Customer Experience is transforming CX from a front-office function into a true growth engine of your company. In this new Product Inspiration Series, we explore how SAP CX brings executional AI into the flow of work by connecting customer signals to real outcomes, powered by the business data and processes that run your organization.
The special character of the SAP CX Product Inspiration Series remains unchanged. Each video:

  • is created by product and industry experts, for fellow experts
  • opens with a short Q&A to set the context—the why, what, and how
  • follows a zero‑slides approach, focusing entirely on the product itself
  • delivers in‑depth explorations and hands‑on demos

entry.title: Re: MF60 can not generate replenishment with 313 movement...
entry.content:
Hello， @Lakshmipathi 

Thanks for your reply.

And our 2 steps means:

1. movement 313  from WM storage location to production supply (non‑WM location) in transit via outbound delivery created by MF60.

2. Production line could  process movement 315 to receive from transit to  production storage location (non‑WM location) .

But now we create the outbound delivery with the movement 411 not 313. So When we process the WM tasks, it directly move into production supply (non‑WM location)  without receiving (MIGO 315).

BTW the effect of 313 is the same as that of 311.

How could we process 2 steps for this business ? 

 

Thanks.

Ying

entry.title: Re: using BAPI_GOODSMVT_CREATE to perform a 102 reversal
entry.content: To have the reversal document (102) correctly reference the original material document in MSEG (fields SJAHR, SMBLN, SMBLP), you need to fill not only REF_DOC_YR, REF_DOC, and REF_DOC_IT in GOODSMVT_ITEM, but also the field MVT_IND (Movement Indicator). MVT_IND tells the system the context of the reversal (e.g., goods issue, goods receipt, transfer posting). Without it, the system cannot generate the reference linkage in MSEG, so the reversal document will post but the reference fields remain blank.

entry.title: Re: How to Analyze My Network Performance

Dear RP_TRIRX,

You may use HTTPWatch as an alternative, as we have had very good experience with this tool for analysing local network performance on the user side.

In case of any questions, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Additionally, please refer to the following recommended approach:

2.1 Browser Network Trace

This is the most effective method to analyse client-side performance.

Steps (Google Chrome / Microsoft Edge):

  1. Open SAP Business ByDesign
  2. Press F12
  3. Navigate to the Network tab
  4. Disable cache
  5. Reload the affected page or execute the slow action

If you consistently facing a performance issue please open a case and provide user, timestamps.

We can then check our server logs, with the given information.

Kind regards,

Didi
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Student access still pending from SAP thought status...
entry.content: Hi Hernest, Welcome to the SAP Community! It’s great to see another student joining the ecosystem. Since your status is already verified by SheerID but the subscription isn't active, it's likely a synchronization delay between SheerID and the SAP Learning system. Here are a few steps you can take: Wait 24-48 Hours: Sometimes the sync takes a bit of time to reflect in the SAP Universal ID or the Learning portal. Check your Email: Look for a confirmation email from SAP (not just SheerID) that says your student access is now active. Don't forget to check the Spam folder. Logout and Login: Completely log out from the SAP Learning site, clear your browser cache, and log back in. This often forces a refresh of your profile permissions. Contact Support: If it’s been more than 2 days, you should reach out to the SAP Learning support team directly. You can usually find a "Support" or "Contact Us" link at the bottom of the SAP Learning Student Zone page. Don't worry, once it's synced, you'll have full access to all the great student resources! Hope this gets resolved soon!

entry.title: Re: CDS for Billing document Business Place
entry.content: The field is available in the billing header extension views (for example, I_BillingDocumentTax or I_BillingDocumentAdditionalFields). These are the CDS views that expose tax‑related and localization fields, including Business Place. Having said that, you will need to either use a tax‑related CDS view that contains it, or build a custom CDS view extension and add it to your form’s data source.

entry.title: In-App Support Configuration Feature in SAP Mobile Start
entry.content:
In today's mobile-first workplace, ensuring users have seamless access to enterprise applications is crucial for productivity and user satisfaction. However, when issues arise, users often find themselves without a clear path to get the help they need. 

Addressing a Critical Gap in User Experience 

One of the most significant challenges organizations face with mobile enterprise applications is the disconnect between users experiencing technical difficulties and the support channels that can actually help them. Users frequently encounter issues such as: 

  • Applications not functioning properly after company-specific setup 
  • Frequent login requirements disrupting their workflow 
  • Missing or unavailable applications they need for their daily tasks 

When these problems occur, frustrated users often turn to channels that aren’t meant for support, such as app store reviews or general feedback forms. This creates a cycle of frustration that impacts both user adoption and overall productivity. 

To bridge this gap, companies can now add end user support information to the SAP Mobile Start app and configure it in SAP Mobile Services. This enhancement transforms how users can access help when they need it most. 

 

How to Configure Support Information 🛠

Support information can be configured through two complementary channels, which can be used independently or together: 

Option 1: Via SAP Mobile Services (Post-Login) 

Administrators can configure support contact information directly in the SAP Mobile Services Cockpit. Navigate to: 

 Mobile Services Cockpit → Mobile Applications → Native/MDK → com.sap.mobile.start → Settings → Client Settings → JSON Storage 

Add the following JSON at the root level of the JSON Storage configuration: 

{ 

  "userSupportConfig": { 

    "displayName": "Acme IT Support", 

    "supportEmail": "helpdesk@acme.com", 

    "supportPhone": "+1-555-123-4567", 

    "supportUrl": "https://support.acme.com" 

  } 

} 

The available fields are: 

Field 

Description 

displayName 

The support team name displayed to users (e.g., "Acme IT Support") 

supportEmail 

Support email address 

supportPhone 

Support phone number 

supportUrl 

Support website URL 

All fields are optional, but at least one contact method (supportEmail, supportPhone, or supportUrl) must be provided for the support entry to appear in the app. 

This configuration is fetched after the user logs in and is displayed in Profile and Settings > Support. 

Note: When configuring the support information, the displayName you enter will be displayed directly to end users within the app. Our recommendation is to follow a clear naming convention using "Company Name + Support" (e.g., "Acme Corp Support" or "TechSolutions Support"). This naming convention helps eliminate any confusion and ensures users know they're contacting the right support team for company-specific SAP Mobile Start issues and configurations. 

Option 2: Via Mobile Device Management (MDM) – Pre-Login and Fallback 

For organizations deploying SAP Mobile Start through an MDM solution (e.g., Microsoft Intune, VMware Workspace ONE, JAMF), support contact information can also be pushed as part of the managed app configuration. This has two key advantages: 

The support entry is available immediately at app launch, even before the user has authenticated – helping users who encounter issues during onboarding. It acts as a fallback in the Settings screen when no Mobile Services configuration has been set. 

Add the following keys to your MDM managed app configuration:  

MDM Key 

Description 

com.sap.mobile.start.support.displayName 

The support team name displayed to users (e.g., "Acme IT Support") 

com.sap.mobile.start.support.email 

Support email address 

com.sap.mobile.start.support.phone 

Support phone number 

com.sap.mobile.start.support.url 

Support website URL 

All fields are optional, but at least one contact method must be provided for the support button to appear. 

Configuration Priority 

When both sources are configured, the following priority applies: 

  • Before login (onboarding screens): MDM configuration is always used. 
  • After login (Settings screen): Mobile Services configuration takes priority. If no Mobile Services configuration is present, the MDM configuration is used as a fallback. 
  • If neither is configured: No support entry is shown. 

This dual-source approach ensures users always have a way to reach IT support, whether they are setting up the app for the first time or using it day-to-day. 

 

Impact on User Experience 📱

Once configured, users can navigate to Profile and Settings > Support to find all configured support channels and contact information. For organizations using Mobile Device Management (MDM), support information is displayed prominently on the first screen, even before users complete the onboarding process 

Profile and Settings screenContact information Details screen

Mobile Device Management (MDM)

This feature represents a significant step forward in creating a more supportive mobile enterprise environment. Instead of leaving users to navigate complex organizational structures to find help, companies can now provide clear, accessible support pathways directly within the mobile experience. 

For IT administrators and support teams, this means receiving support requests through appropriate channels where they can provide meaningful assistance, rather than managing frustrated feedback through app stores or general feedback systems. 

 

-------------------------------------------

I hope you will enjoy these new features in SAP Mobile Start.  
 
For further information on the new topics, please check our SAP Mobile Start documentation. 

SAP Mobile Experience offers intelligent native mobile solutions that help businesses build more efficient, resilient and sustainable end-to-end processes, improving people’s work life wherever they are. 

Visit SAP Mobile Experience Community Page and click “follow” to get the latest development and innovation of our solutions. We look forward to hearing about your experience with setting up the solution in your landscape; please do share your thoughts and comments below. Enter here for additional questions regarding SAP Mobile Experience Applications. 

Want to be notified? Check your profile settings to ensure you have your settings activated.

entry.title: I Don’t Teach Strategy—I Build Strategists with Business Builders
entry.content:
What happens when students stop just learning strategy—and start executing it?  

For many years, I have taught business strategy with a familiar toolkit: frameworks, case studies, and discussions. These approaches are valuable—they build foundational knowledge and help students understand how organizations think and compete. Yet, a persistent question has followed me throughout my teaching journey:

How do we move students beyond understanding strategy… to actually experiencing it?

That question became more relevant when I joined West Chester University in Pennsylvania  and began exploring new ways to connect theory with practice. It was during this time that I introduced Business Builders, an experiential learning simulation supported by SAP University Alliances, into my classroom.

What followed was not just a new teaching tool, but a meaningful shift in how students engaged with strategy.

From Conceptual Learning to Applied Thinking

Traditional instruction often asks students to analyze decisions after they have been made. Simulations, however, ask them to make those decisions themselves—under uncertainty, with incomplete information, and with consequences that unfold in real time.

In my experience, this distinction is critical.

As one student reflected:

“Business Builders made learning strategy, analytics, finance, and decision-making feel hands-on and easy to connect to real situations.”

This observation captures the essence of experiential learning. Strategy is no longer abstract or retrospective; it becomes immediate, dynamic, and personal.

Students begin to see not only what decisions are made, but why they matter.

The Role of Data in Decision-Making

A defining feature of the experience is the integration of data and analytics into the decision process. Through dashboards, real-time scoring, and performance metrics, students are continuously evaluating the outcomes of their choices.

This creates a feedback loop that is both immediate and instructive.

“The dashboards and instant scoring made it easy to see how my choices impacted performance in real time, which helped me learn much faster.”

Rather than relying solely on theoretical reasoning, students are required to interpret data, identify patterns, and adjust their strategies accordingly. In doing so, they begin to develop a mindset that aligns closely with modern business environments—where data-informed decision-making is essential.

Engagement Through Competition and Ownership

Another unexpected, yet powerful, element of the experience is the role of competition. The presence of a leaderboard, updated after each round, introduces a level of urgency and engagement that is difficult to replicate in traditional settings.

“The competitive aspect and leaderboard pushed me to think more strategically and stay engaged.”

What emerges is a sense of ownership. Students are not simply completing an assignment; they are invested in their performance, their decisions, and their outcomes.

This shift—from participation to ownership—changes the nature of learning.

Learning Through Iteration

Perhaps the most valuable aspect of the experience is the opportunity for iteration. Students are not expected to arrive at perfect answers immediately. Instead, they are encouraged to analyze results, adjust their approach, and try again.

One student described this process succinctly:

“The part I enjoyed the most was analyzing the metrics and adjusting my strategy each round.”

This iterative cycle—analyze, decide, adjust—is at the heart of effective strategy execution. It mirrors the realities of business, where decisions are rarely final and adaptability is key.

The Challenge of Complexity

Not all stages of the experience are equally straightforward. As the simulation progresses, the level of complexity increases significantly. In later stages, students are required to build their own analytical views and derive insights without predefined structures.

“Stage 3 is more complex… students need to build the dashboards and find the answers themselves.”

This challenge can be uncomfortable, both for students and instructors. Progress may slow, and not all objectives may be completed within the allotted time.

However, it is precisely in these moments that deeper learning occurs.

Struggle, when guided effectively, becomes a catalyst for growth.

Designing the Learning Experience

One of the lessons I have learned throughout this process is that effective teaching is not only about content—it is also about experience design.

In one session, a student suggested adding background music during the activity to reduce the silence and increase engagement. It was a simple adjustment, yet it transformed the atmosphere of the classroom.

“Don’t forget the music… that makes it more fun.”

Moments like this serve as a reminder that learning environments are co-created. Listening to students and adapting accordingly can significantly enhance the overall experience.

Preparing Students for What Comes Next

Beyond engagement and understanding, the ultimate goal is to prepare students for the realities they will face in their professional careers.

By the end of the semester, students consistently highlighted the practical value of the experience:

“I gained valuable skills in critical thinking, data interpretation, and problem-solving that will be useful in future internships and professional environments.”

These are not abstract competencies. They are essential capabilities for navigating complex, data-driven organizations.

 

A Shift in Perspective

Reflecting on this journey, I recognize that the most significant change has not been in the tools I use, but in the perspective I bring to teaching.

Strategy is no longer something that is simply explained or analyzed.

It is something that is experienced.

Practical Reflections: Lessons Learned and Tips for Educators

For those considering embarking on the Business Builders journey, I would offer a few practical reflections based on my experience in the classroom:

  • Prepare in advance.
    Take the time to go through the materials and understand the flow of the simulations. This preparation may take a few weeks, but it is essential to delivering a strong experience.
  • Play the simulations yourself.
    Experiencing the games firsthand provides valuable insights. If you have a group of students willing to pilot with you, even better—it will help you anticipate questions and challenges.
  • Build familiarity with analytics tools.
    While not mandatory, having additional exposure to SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) can significantly enhance your ability to guide students. The learning resources available through SAP University Alliances are very helpful.
  • Plan your time carefully.
    Each stage can take up to 90 minutes, including preparation and discussion. It is important to allocate sufficient time to avoid rushing the learning process.
  • Integrate it meaningfully into grading.
    In my case, Business Builders represented 15% of the total grade. Participation is key, and there are helpful grading recommendations within the provided materials.
  • Encourage collaboration, even with individual answers.
    While responses are submitted individually, I strongly encourage teamwork and discussion. Peer interaction often helps students who need additional guidance and enriches the overall learning experience.
  • Adapt it to your subject area.
    I use Business Builders in a Business Strategy course, but it is equally effective for data analytics or broader business topics. The flexibility of the scenarios allows for multiple applications.
  • Design the experience—don’t forget the atmosphere.
    Small details matter. Adding music, for example, can make the session more engaging and dynamic. (And yes, a few Latin or upbeat tracks always help. 🎶)

 

Conclusion

The transition from teaching strategy to enabling students to experience it represents a meaningful evolution in business education. It requires preparation, flexibility, and a willingness to embrace complexity. It also requires trust—in the process, and in the students themselves.

In the end, the most impactful learning does not come from observing decisions.

It comes from making them.

 The Agent Video teaser  

Have you had the opportunity to use Business Builders? I’d be interested to hear about your experience and any lessons learned.

entry.title: Elevate Your Commerce Strategy: AI-Focused Live Sessions for SAP Commerce Cloud
entry.content:
AI is transforming the way we approach e-commerce, from streamlining development to revolutionizing customer engagement. To ensure you have the most current knowledge, we are highlighting three essential instructor-led live sessions that showcase the practical application of AI within SAP Commerce Cloud.

Whether you are looking to explore our brand-new offerings or want to stay current with the latest toolkit updates, these sessions provide the insights you need to stay competitive.

1. Applying Joule for Consultants in SAP Commerce Cloud Development

This brand-new session focuses on Joule for Consultants (J4C). This isn't just another AI chatbot; it is a specialized, context-aware application designed specifically to assist experts in the development and configuration of SAP Commerce Cloud.

  • Move Beyond Basics: Learn how J4C provides deep technical support for complex implementation tasks, moving far beyond standard AI assistant features.
  • Accelerate Delivery: See practical, hands-on examples of how to use J4C to speed up development cycles and ensure your configurations align with SAP best practices.
  • Solve Real Challenges: Discover how to apply AI-driven techniques to solve common implementation hurdles that consultants and developers face every day.
  • Ideal for: Consultants, Developers, and Architects looking for a competitive edge in project delivery.

2. Introducing the SAP CX AI Toolkit for SAP Commerce Cloud

The SAP CX AI Toolkit is constantly evolving. While this is an established session, it remains a "must-attend" because it is continuously updated to include the very latest features and capabilities released by SAP.

  • Your Foundation for AI: Get a clear business overview of the toolkit’s core features and its specific relevance to SAP Commerce Cloud operations.
  • Stay Up to Date: We dive into the most recent updates, ensuring you understand the latest tools available to optimize your digital commerce strategies.
  • Efficiency & Experience: Learn how the toolkit’s evolving functionalities can be used to enhance customer experiences and support more efficient back-office operations.
  • Ideal for: Business Users, Analysts, and Consultants who need to stay informed on the current state of SAP’s AI tools.

3. Leveraging AI Agents in the SAP CX AI Toolkit for SAP Commerce Cloud

Our second brand-new session takes a deep dive into the world of Autonomous AI Agents. This session explores how specialized agents within the SAP CX AI Toolkit can take over complex tasks, allowing your team to focus on higher-level strategy.

  • Automation in Action: Experience practical demonstrations of the Digital Service Agent, the Shopping Agent, and the Catalog Optimization Agent.
  • Practical Application: Understand exactly how these agents integrate with SAP Commerce Cloud to automate workflows and enhance user engagement.
  • Strategic Implementation: Gain the insights needed to deploy these intelligent tools effectively, ensuring they drive operational efficiency from day one.
  • Ideal for: Developers, Architects, and Business Users interested in the cutting edge of commerce automation.

Why Attend a Live Session?

These sessions are designed for those who want a guided, interactive learning experience. You will:

  • See Live Demonstrations: Watch these AI tools in action within a commerce environment.
  • Get Expert Guidance: Our instructors provide context and clarity on how these tools can be applied.
  • Stay Current: With AI moving at lightning speed, these sessions are the most efficient way to keep your skills up to date.

Register Today

Don't fall behind in the AI revolution. Explore these sessions on the SAP Learning site and reserve your spot in our next live broadcast.

  • Applying Joule for Consultants in SAP Commerce Cloud Development
  • Introducing the SAP CX AI Toolkit for SAP Commerce Cloud 
  • Leveraging AI Agents in the SAP CX AI Toolkit for SAP Commerce Cloud

Empower your team and transform your commerce strategy with the latest AI innovations from SAP.

Happy learning!

entry.title: Re: LSMW Functions in SAP S/4 Hana Version 2025
entry.content:
Below would be the recommended transition approach:

1) Inventory Existing LSMW Projects
Classify them into initial migration vs ongoing updates.

2) Map to MYD Objects
For initial migration, identify the equivalent migration objects in MYD/DMC.

3) Replace Recordings
For update scenarios, replace recordings with released APIs or staging table migration objects.

4) Workflow/Approval Needs
If updates require governance, consider SAP MDG or a lightweight workflow using SAP Build Process Automation.

5) Testing
Run pilot migrations in a sandbox to validate that MYD objects cover your LSMW scope.

I’ve resolved the issue.

  • The first problem was fixed by migrating to version 2211.9 jdk21.
  • For the second problem, I added the following configuration in spartacus-configuration.module.ts:
{ provide: AuthHttpHeaderService, useExisting: AsmAuthHttpHeaderService, }

This resolved the remaining issue.

Best regards,

Stefania

entry.title: Re: AM_ASSET - Archiving for a specific asset number
entry.content: Yes, you can restrict archiving by specific asset numbers in the write program selection screen of AM_ASSET. However, the system will only archive those assets if they also meet the defined residence time and retention rules. If they don’t, they remain in the database until the retention period expires.

entry.title: Re: Error in Post Freight Cost Allocation
entry.content:
Thank you for your question.

Looking at the message on the screenshots and the description you provided, the issue is most likely not the charge type definition itself, but the missing G/L account determination for the valuation class used by the new TM charge type ZSB0 during freight cost allocation posting. In TM freight settlement, the system uses the MM integration settings of the charge type to derive a service product, and th assigned valuation class is then used in the FI account determination when freight cost accruals are posted. For reference, see Configuring Transportation Management and Settling Freight Charges

Your screenshot shows that ZSB0 is assigned with valuation class 2002 in Assign MM integration Settings to Charge Types. When the Freight Cost Allocation document is released, the system tries to determine the account for account key TM1. Note that TM1 is the Freight Clearing Account key. Therefore, if the system finds no valid account determination line for the relevant combination such as chart of accounts, valuation grouping, valuation class, and transaction key TM1, your posting will fail and generate that error you are seeing. See SAP’s best practice on FR1_Freight Clearing for more details.

What that means is, you can use TM charge type for fuel costs in your scenario, provided you have completed its MM and FI integration.

So, based on the error you are facing, you seem to have configured the new charge type ZSB0 on the TM side, but the FI automatic account determination for TM1 does not appear to be complete for the 2002 derived valuation class. This explains why your Freight Cost Allocation posting stops at account determination.

See the following reference for more details.

2595334 - Receiving error WS043 "Error in account determination for account key &" when creating settlement - SAP for Me

2959280 - Customizing for Billing of Freight Costs - Integration with Transportation Management - SAP for Me

I recommend you check the following:

  1. Verify the charge type integration. Although from your screenshot this looks correct, ensure you confirm ZSB0 is assigned correctly in Assign MM Integration Settings to Charge Types with valuation class = 2002, account assignment category = J, and condition type = your intended purchase condition type.
  2.  Check the missing FI account determination for TM1 in Automatic Account Determination 

   and review the entries for transaction key TM1. You need to confirm that there is a valid line for the same valuation logic used by your new charge type, especially valuation class 2002. If your current TM1 entries only cover other valuation classes, the new fuel charge type will fail exactly as you have it.

  1. Compare one of your standard freight charge type that posts successfully,

  look at the valuation class, then compare it with ZSB0. If the standard charge points to a valuation class already mapped under TM1 and ZSB0 points to 2002 without a corresponding TM1 line, that will confirm the root cause.

  1. Once the TM1 account determination is complete for the relevant valuation class, reprocess your Freight Cost Allocation document.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: New Learning Journey: Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance
entry.content:
Why This Learning Journey Matters

Artificial intelligence is rapidly reshaping how organizations operate, from automating routine processes to supporting smarter decision-making. Finance professionals who understand how to apply AI in real business scenarios are becoming essential change agents.

This intermediate-level learning journey is built for learners who already understand core finance processes and are ready to take the next step: applying SAP Business AI capabilities directly within SAP Finance scenarios.

What You’ll Learn

This learning journey provides a guided, hands-on exploration of SAP’s latest AI innovations for finance, including:

  • SAP Joule in Finance
    See how SAP’s AI-powered digital assistant supports day-to-day finance tasks and insights.
  • SAP Joule Agents
    Learn how specialized AI agents can assist with more complex and cross-functional finance activities.
  • SAP Joule for Consultants
    Discover AI-driven tools that help consultants analyze, explain, and design finance solutions more effectively.
  • SAP Business Data Cloud in Finance
    Explore how intelligent, harmonized data enables advanced reporting, planning, and decision support.

Throughout the journey, learners follow practical examples and concrete finance use cases, bridging the gap between theory and real-world application.

Who Should Take This Learning Journey?

This learning journey is ideal for Finance consultants and solution architects, as well as Finance business experts interested in AI-driven innovation. Basic knowledge of SAP financial business processes is recommended to get the most value from the content. If you’re new to AI in finance, SAP recommends starting with a foundational journey such as Discovering SAP Business AI Capabilities for SAP Finance, before continuing with this more advanced, application-focused journey. 

Get Started Today

👉Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance

Upskill, experiment, and position yourself at the forefront of AI-powered finance transformation.

entry.title: In-process quality inspections with SAP Digital Manufacturing and POD 2.0 integrated with S/4HANA QM
entry.content:
Business context

In-process quality inspections are checks or evaluations performed at various stages during the manufacturing or assembly process, rather than waiting until final inspection. These inspections typically occur on the shop floor—the area where actual production takes place.

SAP Digital Manufacturing (SAP DM) is a cloud-based solution that enables real-time visibility, control, and optimization of manufacturing processes. Its integration with SAP S/4HANA Quality Management (QM) brings together manufacturing execution and enterprise quality data, streamlining quality assurance across the production lifecycle. Through this integration, quality inspection results and defect records captured on the shop floor in SAP DM can be synchronized directly with S/4HANA QM. This ensures data consistency, faster feedback loops, and informed decision-making, helping organizations achieve higher product quality, improved compliance, and more efficient manufacturing execution.

For recording inspection results in SAP DM integrated with S/4HANA inspection lots, you can use the Production Operator Dashboard (POD) in SAP DM.  With Release 2605 SAP DM delivers a new framework for the definition and configuration of the PODs, called POD 2.0. In this context, there is also a redesigned Plugin for inspection results recording available, which significantly enhances usability and offers new features.

This blog focuses on quality inspections in SAP DM integrated with S/4HANA QM inspection lots. It does not cover data collections with SAP DM, nonconformance logging, or AI-assisted visual inspections.

Prerequisites

You must be using SAP ERP or an S/4HANA edition (private or public) to maintain QM master data and enable the corresponding integration scenarios with SAP DM. For details, please see these SAP Help Portal pages and related links in the diagram. 
For S/4HANA and SAP ERP:  https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-digital-manufacturing/integration-guide/overview-integration-with-sap-s-4hana-sap-erp-for-discrete-industries
For S/4HANA Public Cloud: Overview Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for Discrete Industries | SAP Help Portal 
Prebuilt connectors and APIs enable standard, real-time data exchange between SAP DM and S/4HANA.

S/4HANA is the leading system for quality planning; SAP DM is used for execution of quality inspections as in-process controls during production or process order execution. Once the production or process order is released in S/4HANA, an in-process inspection lot (inspection type 03) is generated automatically, and the order along with the relevant inspection operations and inspection characteristics are downloaded to SAP DM.

Master data and configuration in S/4HANA

  • Ensure inspection type 03 is active for the relevant material master records in S/4HANA.
  • Assign inspection characteristics to routing operations or recipe phases/operations as appropriate.
  • Optional: enable inspection points in the routing/recipe header QM data when multiple inspections of the same parameter are required.

Configuration in SAP Digital Manufacturing

After you set up the integration scenarios, most configuration is complete. If you use inspection points within S/4HANA QM inspection lots, make sure you mirror the inspection-point identification settings from S/4HANA in your SAP DM configuration table per plant (SAP DM app: “Configure Inspection Points”).

Limitations for inspection-point integration with SAP DM:

  • Only the “free inspection point” type is supported for integration with SAP DM at this time
  • Inspection points are currently supported only for integration with S/4HANA Private Cloud and SAP ERP

Setting up the QM POD Plugin in POD 2.0

In order to use the redesigned POD Plugin for inspection results recording, you must do the following (as of SAP DM Release 2605):

In the “Manage POD 2.0” app:

  1. Select a POD template.
  2. Create a new page (for example, name it “Quality Inspection”).
  3. Drag and drop the QM POD Plugin “Quality Inspection Characteristic Table” into the new page.
  4. Save the POD.

(Figure 1: Quality Inspection POD PlugIn in Manage POD 2.0 App)

For general information about POD 2.0 features and configuration options, review the POD 2.0 blog post series in the SAP Community
https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blog-posts-by-sap/new-pod-2-0-framework-the-new-generation-of-sap-digital-manufacturing/ba-p/14324719

Recording inspection results with SAP DM POD 2.0

A practical process flow example for order execution with in-process checks may look like this:

  1. A production order with an inspection lot is created and released in S/4HANA.
  2. SAP DM receives the order; the Production Supervisor releases the order in SAP DM and the operator follows digital instructions and completes in-process checks in the POD.
  3. Inspection results are recorded in the POD in SAP DM for one or more operations / work centers.
  4. Optionally, exceptions or nonconformances can be communicated back to S/4HANA QM - for example, by logging nonconformances in SAP DM, which can trigger the creation of a production defect in S/4HANA.
  5. S/4HANA uses the input from the shop floor to update the quality status of materials, make a usage decision based on the entered inspection results, and maintain compliance records.

Enhanced usability for inspection results recording

To reduce the number of clicks for operators and quality technicians on the shop floor, the UI of the POD Plugin for inspection results recording has been redesigned and optimized. In now provides faster data entry and full visibility into the status of inspection operations.
New or improved features include:

  • An overview of all inspection results on one screen (including qualitative inspection characteristics) via the “View Summary” button.
  • A “Save and Next” button that allows users to jump directly to the next inspection characteristic with one click.
  • A separate side panel that lists all inspection characteristics for easier navigation and scrolling.

(Figure 2: New user interface for inspection results recording in POD)

 
New features for QM inspection results recording

Inspection results can be entered manually by the operator or quality technician, or automatically via the Production Connector and an associated Production Process that collects data from an electronic measurement device. Some new features have been added to the QM POD 2.0 Plugin:

  1. You can enter an inspection description (comment) on header level and single results level for each individual inspection characteristic
  2. Depending on the inspection characteristic configuration in S/4HANA QM, the system can enforce mandatory comments (i.e., inspection description/documentation required resp. required if result is rejected). 

(Figure 3: Mandatory comments can be entered at inspection characteristic header level)

Other functional improvements available across POD frameworks (available in POD 1.0 and POD 2.0):

  • When results are transferred back to the inspection lot in S/4HANA, the operator/inspector name is mapped to the “Inspector” field (previously it was only the eight-digit username).
  • The “Changed date & time” field reflects the date and time when the inspection characteristic result was completed, not when it was initially transferred from SAP DM.

The existing XSLT mapping provides extensibility—for example, to automatically complete inspection results (status “5”) when sending data from SAP DM to S/4HANA. For details, see the SAP Help Portal: Enabling Auto Valuation and Completion of Inspection Characteristics | SAP Help Portal

Conclusion

In-process quality inspections let manufacturers detect and address issues early during production, improving product quality, lowering costs, and ensuring regulatory compliance.
SAP Digital Manufacturing modernizes in-process inspection execution and, when integrated with S/4HANA QM, delivers a seamless and auditable flow from production order creation to inspection and product release decisions. The POD 2.0 QM Plugin improves operator usability and tightens the integration between shop-floor execution and enterprise quality management in S/4HANA.

Limitations

When using the SAP Digital Manufacturing integration with Cloud ERP, please consider the following known limitations:

  • Only inspection lots of type 03 are supported in SAP DM.
  • Only 100% inspection is supported; flexible sampling sizes are not supported.
  • Only single result values for inspection characteristics are supported (no summarized results support).
  • Inspection lots using inspection points are supported only for integration with S/4HANA Private Cloud, on-premise, and SAP ERP (ECC).

For further details, see SAP Note 3379404 - SAP Digital Manufacturing: Restrictions and Limitations Note - SAP for Me

References

  • POD 2.0 blog: New POD 2.0 framework — the new generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing (SAP Community)
    https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blog-posts-by-sap/new-pod-2-0-framework-the-new-generation-of-sap-digital-manufacturing/ba-p/14324719
  • SAP Help Portal — Recording Quality Inspection Results
    Recording Quality Inspection Results | SAP Help Portal
  • SAP Help Portal —  Configuring POD 2.0
    Configure Manufacturing Plugins in Your POD | SAP Help Portal
  • SAP Video — QM In-Process Inspection with SAP Digital Manufacturing (with POD 1.0)
    SAP Video - QM In-Process Inspection with SAP Digital Manufacturing

entry.title: Re: SAC Reporting Dynamic calculation Between Versions
entry.content:
@shiva1233 

Since you have created Selection V1 & Selection V3 both as Restricted Measures with version input controls, restrict the date as in these restricted measure to be current and previous year using dynamic date restriction. Finally use a calculated measure for variance. Keep constant selection on in restricted measure and remove version from column axis of your table.

Hope this helps !

Nikhil
